assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about feedback mechanisms is most accurate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Negative feedback mechanisms return a system to its set point.
Positive feedback mechanisms bring a system closer to its set point.
Negative feedback mechanisms move a system further from its set point.
Positive feedback mechanisms maintain a system at its set point.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about feedback loops is most accurate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Positive feedback loops amplify changes to the set point of a system.
Negative feedback loops amplify changes to the set point of a system.
Positive feedback loops reverse changes to the set point of a system.
Negative feedback loops maintain changes to the set point of a system.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following correctly describes how positive and negative feedback mechanisms are similar?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both feedback mechanisms occur in response to stimuli.
Both feedback mechanisms amplify changes to the set point of a system.
Both feedback mechanisms act to reverse changes in a system.
Both feedback mechanisms move a system further from its starting point.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following correctly describes how positive and negative feedback are different?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Negative feedback reverses changes in a system, while positive feedback amplifies changes in a system.
Positive feedback occurs only in response to stimuli, while negative feedback can occur in the absence of stimuli.
Negative feedback requires the detection of stimuli, while positive feedback functions independently of stimuli.
Positive feedback moves a system closer to its set point, while negative feedback moves a system further from its set point.

assessment_items.question_content:
The endocrine system maintains homeostasis in the body through the release of various hormones. For example, the pancreas releases the hormone insulin to help regulate blood glucose homeostasis. 

**Which of the following best describes the role of insulin in regulating blood glucose homeostasis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Insulin is released in response to high blood glucose levels. This release triggers a negative feedback loop that returns blood glucose levels back to normal.
Insulin is released in response to low blood glucose levels. This release triggers a negative feedback loop that returns blood glucose levels back to normal.
Insulin is released in response to high blood glucose levels. This release triggers a positive feedback loop that further increases blood glucose levels.
Insulin is released in response to low blood glucose levels. This release triggers a positive feedback loop that further decreases blood glucose levels.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: Adapted from BrianMSweis [CC BY-SA 3.0], via Wikimedia Commons*

In response to stress, the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis coordinates the release of the steroid hormone cortisol, as summarized in the figure above. Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are a class of synthetic steroid hormones that are commonly used in the long-term treatment of persistent asthma.

**Assuming that ICS function in the same way as naturally occurring cortisol, it is most likely that long-term, high-dose ICS use would**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The diagram is labeled HPA axis. There are 3 circles drawn on an diagonal from top to bottom with the largest circle at the top labeled hypothalamus. There is an arrow pointing from hypothalamus to the next circle labeled anterior pituitary. Next to the arrow is the label CRH. From the anterior pituitary there is an arrow pointing to the last circle labeled adrenal cortex. Next to the arrow from the anterior pituitary is the label ACTH. From the adrenal cortex there is an arrow pointing at cortisol production. From cortisol production there is a line that goes up and divides with one line going towards the anterior pituitary and the other line going towards the hypothalamus. Where the line branches is the caption High cortisol levels, and at the end of each line that branches there is a negative symbol.

choices.content: reduce cortisol production
stimulate CRH production
stimulate ACTH production
stimulate cortisol production

assessment_items.question_content:
The figure below depicts how the hormone oxytocin functions during the onset of labor.


[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: OpenStax [CC BY 4.0], via Wikimedia Commons*

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the figure above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: An image of a baby inside the uterus with the head of the baby at the cervix. Outside the image are a series of arrows with captions between the arrows. Starting at the top of the image is the caption brain stimulates pituitary gland to secrete oxytocin with an arrow pointing to the caption oxytocin carried in bloodstream to uterus. The next arrow points to the caption oxytocin stimulates uterine contractions and pushes baby towards cervix. The next arrow points to the caption head of baby pushes against cervix and then an arrow points to the caption nerve impulses from cervix transmitted to brain. The next arrow points back to the top of the image.

choices.content: The frequency of uterine contractions increases as labor progresses.
The frequency of uterine contractions decreases as labor progresses.
Oxytocin levels in the bloodstream decrease as labor progresses.
Oxytocin levels in the bloodstream remain constant as labor progresses.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios describes a positive feedback mechanism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: After childbirth, high levels of prolactin induce lactation in the mother. During nursing, infant suckling triggers the release of more prolactin, which further stimulates lactation.
After eating, blood glucose levels rise, stimulating the release of insulin from the pancreas. Insulin causes cells to uptake glucose from the blood, lowering blood glucose levels.

Several hours after a meal, blood glucose levels begin to drop, prompting the pancreas to secrete glucagon. Glucagon causes the liver to convert stored glycogen into glucose, raising blood glucose levels.
During exercise, an increase in body temperature causes the body to produce sweat. As the sweat vaporizes, the body cools down due to evaporative cooling.
